section.title: The Northern Campaign
section.sectionRationale: The conquest sweeps north. A vast coalition under Jabin of Hazor gathers with horses and chariots, an army like the sand of the seashore, but God tells Joshua not to fear and delivers them. Joshua takes Hazor and the northern cities, even the feared Anakim, and at last the whole land is taken and the land has rest from war. It is one movement, the northern campaign, which completes the conquest and shows God faithful to give the whole land, exactly as He promised Moses.
section.thesis: Against the largest, best-armed enemy yet, God again wins the victory and hands over the land, so that Joshua leaves nothing undone of all the LORD commanded Moses; even the dreaded giants fall, and the God who promised the land proves faithful to give it, granting His people rest from war.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the northern coalition gathers and God promises victory, verses 1 to 9; the fall of Hazor and the northern cities, verses 10 to 15; the whole land taken, including the Anakim, verses 16 to 22; and the land at rest, verse 23.

units.label: The northern coalition and God's promise
units.note: do not be afraid; by this time tomorrow I will deliver them

verses.text: Now when Jabin king of Hazor heard about these things, he sent word to Jobab king of Madon; to the kings of Shimron and Achshaph;
to the kings of the north in the mountains, in the Arabah south of Chinnereth, in the foothills, and in Naphoth-dor to the west;
to the Canaanites in the east and west; to the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, and Jebusites in the hill country; and to the Hivites at the foot of Hermon in the land of Mizpah.
So these kings came out with all their armies, a multitude as numerous as the sand on the seashore, along with a great number of horses and chariots.
All these kings joined forces and encamped at the waters of Merom to fight against Israel.
Then the LORD said to Joshua, “Do not be afraid of them, for by this time tomorrow I will deliver all of them slain before Israel. You are to hamstring their horses and burn up their chariots.”
So by the waters of Merom, Joshua and his whole army came upon them suddenly and attacked them,

and the LORD delivered them into the hand of Israel, who struck them down and pursued them all the way to Greater Sidon and Misrephoth-maim, and eastward as far as the Valley of Mizpeh. They struck them down, leaving no survivors.
Joshua treated them as the LORD had told him; he hamstrung their horses and burned up their chariots.

units.label: The fall of Hazor and the northern cities
units.note: leaving nothing undone of all the LORD commanded Moses

verses.text: At that time Joshua turned back and captured Hazor and put its king to the sword, because Hazor was formerly the head of all these kingdoms.
The Israelites put everyone in Hazor to the sword, devoting them to {{cherem|destruction}}. Nothing that breathed remained, and Joshua burned down Hazor itself.
Joshua captured all these kings and their cities and put them to the sword. He devoted them to destruction, as Moses the LORD’s servant had commanded.
Yet Israel did not burn any of the cities built on their mounds, except Hazor, which Joshua burned.
The Israelites took for themselves all the plunder and livestock of these cities, but they put all the people to the sword until they had completely destroyed them, not sparing anyone who breathed.
As the LORD had commanded His servant Moses, so Moses commanded Joshua. That is what Joshua did, leaving nothing undone of all that the LORD had commanded Moses.

units.label: The whole land taken, including the Anakim
units.note: it was of the LORD to harden their hearts

verses.text: So Joshua took this entire region: the hill country, all the Negev, all the land of Goshen, the western foothills, the Arabah, and the mountains of Israel and their foothills,
from Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir, as far as Baal-gad in the Valley of Lebanon at the foot of Mount Hermon. He captured all their kings and struck them down, putting them to death.
Joshua waged war against all these kings for a long period of time.
No city made peace with the Israelites except the Hivites living in Gibeon; all others were taken in battle.

For it was of the LORD to {{chazaq|harden}} their hearts to engage Israel in battle, so that they would be set apart for destruction and would receive no mercy, being annihilated as the LORD had commanded Moses.

At that time Joshua proceeded to eliminate the Anakim from the hill country of Hebron, Debir, and Anab, and from all the hill country of Judah and of Israel. Joshua devoted them to destruction, along with their cities.
No Anakim were left in the land of the Israelites; only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod did any survive.

units.label: The land at rest
units.note: then the land had rest from war

verses.text: So Joshua took the entire land, in keeping with all that the LORD had spoken to Moses. And Joshua gave it as an inheritance to Israel according to the allotments to their tribes. Then the land had {{shaqat|rest}} from war.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted irrevocably to the LORD, here by destruction. As in the south, the northern cities are placed under the cherem, carried out just as Moses the LORD's servant had commanded. The repeated ban frames the conquest as God's judgment under His authority, and the note that Israel kept the plunder while devoting the people shows the ban fell on Canaan's guilty inhabitants, not on greed for spoil.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm; here in the causative sense, to harden or strengthen. The narrator says it was of the LORD to chazaq the hearts of the kings to fight Israel, echoing the hardening of Pharaoh, Exodus 9:12. God's sovereignty stands behind even the enemies' resolve to fight, bringing His righteous judgment on nations already ripe for it, while their own hostility remains truly their own.

shaqat.gloss: To be quiet, at rest, undisturbed. The chapter closes with the land shaqat, at rest from war, the goal toward which the conquest moved. This rest, secured by God's faithfulness to His promise, is a foretaste of the deeper rest to which Joshua's rest points, the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God in Christ, Hebrews 4:9.

basic.context: The conquest now turns north. Jabin, king of the powerful city of Hazor, rallies a huge coalition of northern kings with horses and chariots, an army as numerous as the sand of the seashore, far bigger than anything Israel has faced. But God tells Joshua the same thing He has said all along: Do not be afraid, for by this time tomorrow I will deliver them. God gives the victory, Joshua hamstrings the horses and burns the chariots so Israel will not trust in them, and Hazor is taken and burned. City by city the north falls. Even the Anakim, the giants who terrified Israel a generation earlier, are driven out. At last the whole land is taken, given to the tribes as their inheritance, and the land has rest from war. The question underneath is: will God keep His promise to give the whole land?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, summarizing the completion of the conquest. Its refrain is that Joshua did all that the LORD commanded Moses, tying the whole campaign to God's faithfulness to His word. The hard note that God hardened the kings' hearts must be read alongside the wider biblical teaching on God's sovereignty and human responsibility, and the herem, as before, as His righteous judgment on Canaan. The rest at the end points beyond itself to a greater rest.
basic.originalLanguages: The northern cities are placed under the cherem, devoted to destruction as Moses commanded. God chazaq, hardens, the kings' hearts to fight, bringing His judgment. And the land at last has shaqat, rest, from war, a foretaste of the greater rest.
basic.typology: The defeat of the giants who once terrified Israel, and the securing of rest in the land, point to Christ, who overcomes the powers that terrify His people and brings them into a rest that cannot be lost. The hamstrung horses and burned chariots picture trust in God rather than in worldly might.
basic.christ: The Anakim, the giants whose report once made Israel's heart melt, are finally destroyed, showing that no enemy is too great for the God who fights for His people. So Christ defeats the powers that hold His people in fear, and He brings them into the rest the land only foreshadowed, the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, Hebrews 4.
basic.god: God is faithful to His promise, giving the whole land just as He spoke to Moses. He is sovereign over the hearts of kings and the outcome of battles. He is the warrior who delivers a small people from a vast, well-armed enemy. And He is the giver of rest, bringing His people into the settled inheritance He swore to their fathers.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 13 records the giants who once terrified Israel, now destroyed. Psalm 20 says some trust in chariots, but we trust in the LORD. Deuteronomy 7 promised God would clear the nations before Israel. And Hebrews 4 shows the land's rest as a shadow of the rest in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The conquest is completed as God gives the north, destroys the feared giants, and grants the land rest from war, keeping every promise He made to Moses. It is the fulfillment of the pledge to Abraham, and the rest secured points forward to the greater and lasting rest won by Christ.

context.literary: Chapter 11 mirrors chapter 10: a coalition gathers, God says do not be afraid and promises victory, and the enemy falls, followed by a summary of conquered territory. Together the two chapters cover the south and the north, and chapter 11 closes the conquest narrative proper. The repeated as the LORD commanded Moses, verses 12, 15, 20, and 23, is the theological signature of the section, stressing Joshua's complete obedience and God's faithfulness. The closing note, the land had rest from war, provides the resolution before the book turns to dividing the land.
context.historical: Hazor was the largest and most important city in the north, described here as formerly the head of all these kingdoms, and archaeology confirms it as a major Canaanite center. Horses and chariots gave the northern kings a formidable military advantage on the plains, which is why God had Israel hamstring the horses and burn the chariots rather than adopt them, keeping Israel from trusting in such power, Deuteronomy 17:16. The Anakim were a people of great stature whose presence had terrified the earlier generation of spies, Numbers 13:28-33; their defeat here reverses that failure of faith. The remnant surviving in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod anticipates the later Philistine giants such as Goliath.
context.canonical: The chapter completes the fulfillment of the land promise given to Abraham and renewed to Moses, underscored by the refrain that Joshua left nothing undone of all the LORD commanded. God hardening the kings' hearts stands in the biblical line of divine sovereignty over human opposition, as with Pharaoh, serving His righteous judgment. The destruction of the Anakim answers the faithless report of Numbers 13, showing what trust in God accomplishes. And the land's rest from war looks forward to the theme of rest that Hebrews 4 develops, the shadow of the eternal rest secured in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the completion of the conquest in the north, to show God faithful to give the whole land just as He promised Moses, to display His power over the greatest enemy and the feared giants, and to bring the narrative to rest with the land at peace and apportioned to the tribes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the God-commanded completion of a unique conquest, including the herem and the divine hardening; it does not prescribe holy war or its methods for later peoples. What endures is its theology: God keeps His promises fully, He is sovereign over kings and battles, He is worthy of trust rather than reliance on worldly might such as chariots, and the rest He gives foreshadows the greater rest in Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that the LORD hardened the hearts of the kings so they would fight and be destroyed?

views.label: Divine sovereignty serving righteous judgment
views.case: As with Pharaoh, God's hardening confirms and gives over hearts already set against Him, bringing His just judgment on nations whose sin was long ripe, Genesis 15:16; the kings choose to fight, yet God sovereignly overrules their choice for His purposes, so their guilt is real and His justice is upheld.

views.label: A judicial handing-over of persistent rebels
views.case: The hardening is best understood as God giving these peoples over to the hostile course they were already bent on, Romans 1:24-28, so that mercy, though genuinely available as Rahab and Gibeon show, is withdrawn from those who persist in defiance; the text stresses that they were annihilated as the LORD had commanded, framing it as judgment, not caprice.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that God is sovereign even over His enemies and that His judgment is just, while human hostility remains truly the people's own; the passage warns against presuming on God and drives us to the mercy still open to all who turn to Him.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the theology of the northern campaign. City after city is placed under the cherem, devoted to destruction, and the narrator is careful to say it was done as Moses the LORD's servant had commanded, so the ban is framed as obedient execution of God's judgment, not Israelite initiative; the note that Israel kept the plunder while devoting the people shows the ban fell on Canaan's guilty inhabitants, not on greed for spoil. Then comes a striking statement: it was of the LORD to chazaq, to harden, the hearts of the kings to fight Israel, the same verb and the same mystery as the hardening of Pharaoh, so that God's sovereignty stands behind even the enemies' resolve, bringing His righteous judgment on peoples already ripe for it while their hostility remains their own. Finally the chapter, and the whole conquest, comes to rest: the land shaqat, it was quiet, at rest from war. That rest is the goal the campaign aimed at, secured by God's faithfulness to His promise, and it is a foretaste of the deeper Sabbath rest that Joshua could not finally give but Christ does, Hebrews 4:8-9. Devotion, hardening, rest: the words show God judging, ruling, and fulfilling His promise.

typology.type: The destruction of the Anakim, the giants who once terrified Israel
typology.fulfillment: Christ overcoming the powers that hold His people in fear, so that no enemy is too great for the God who fights for them, Hebrews 2:14-15 and 1 John 4:4.

typology.type: The land at rest from war, the goal of the conquest
typology.fulfillment: The Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God, which Joshua's rest foreshadowed and Christ secures, Hebrews 4:8-10.

typology.type: Hamstrung horses and burned chariots, refusing to trust in worldly might
typology.fulfillment: God's people trusting in the LORD rather than in chariots and horses, and boasting only in Christ, Psalm 20:7 and 2 Corinthians 10:4.

study.christ: Two threads reach toward Christ. First, the Anakim, the giants whose very name had made the earlier generation's hearts melt so that they refused to enter the land, Numbers 13, are here rooted out; the enemy that once seemed unbeatable falls before the God who fights for His people. So the Lord Jesus takes on the greatest enemies of all, sin, death, and the devil, and through death destroys the one who had the power of death, that He might free those who all their lives were held in slavery by the fear of death, Hebrews 2:14-15. Second, the chapter ends where the whole conquest was heading: the land had rest from war. Yet Hebrews tells us Joshua did not give the people the final rest, for God still speaks of another rest to come; so there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, entered by faith in Christ, Hebrews 4:8-10. The greater Joshua defeats the giants we could never defeat and leads us into the rest that no enemy can ever again disturb.
study.god: The God of this chapter is faithful, sovereign, and mighty to save. He keeps His promise to the letter, giving the entire land just as He had spoken to Moses, so that Joshua leaves nothing undone; the refrain as the LORD commanded stamps the whole campaign as the outworking of God's faithful word. He is sovereign even over His enemies, so absolutely that the kings' resolve to fight serves His righteous judgment. He is the warrior who delivers a foot-army from a vast host of horses and chariots, and He teaches His people to trust Him rather than such power. And He is the giver of rest, bringing His people at last into the settled inheritance He swore to their fathers. This is a God whose promises never fail, whose judgments are just, and whose salvation reaches its goal in the rest He grants His people.

crossReferences.note: The Anakim once terrified the spies into unbelief; here they are destroyed, reversing that failure of faith.
Israel's king was not to multiply horses; hamstringing the horses and burning the chariots kept Israel trusting God, not military power.
Some trust in chariots and horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD, the lesson of the burned chariots.
Christ destroys the one who had the power of death and frees those enslaved by fear, the greater conquest of the giants.
Joshua did not give the final rest; a Sabbath rest remains for the people of God in Christ.

study.oneStory: The conquest reaches its climax as God gives the north, topples mighty Hazor, destroys the feared giants, and brings the whole land to rest, keeping every promise He made to Moses. It is the fulfillment of the ancient pledge to Abraham that his descendants would possess the land. Yet the rest Joshua secures is only a shadow: it points beyond itself to the greater Joshua, Jesus, who defeats the enemies we cannot and leads His people into the Sabbath rest that lasts forever.

quiz.q: What was different about the northern enemy Israel faced?

options.text: It was a huge coalition with horses and chariots, an army as numerous as the sand of the seashore.
options.note: Verses 4 to 5 stress the vast size and military power of the northern alliance.

options.text: It was a small band easily defeated.
options.note: The opposite: it was the largest and best-armed force Israel had yet faced.

options.text: It refused to fight and surrendered at once.
options.note: The coalition gathered specifically to fight Israel at the waters of Merom, not to surrender.

options.text: It was made up entirely of the Gibeonites.
options.note: The Gibeonites had made peace; this was a coalition of northern kings under Jabin of Hazor.

quiz.q: Why did God have Israel hamstring the horses and burn the chariots?

options.text: So Israel would trust in the LORD rather than in military power.
options.note: Destroying the horses and chariots kept Israel from relying on worldly might, as verse 6 and 9 show.

options.text: Because the horses and chariots were diseased.
options.note: There is no mention of disease; the point was to keep Israel from trusting in such power.

options.text: Because Israel could not afford to feed the horses.
options.note: The reason was spiritual, trusting God not chariots, not the cost of feeding horses.

options.text: To sell them to the Gibeonites for profit.
options.note: They were destroyed, not sold; the aim was reliance on God, not profit.

quiz.q: What happened to the Anakim, the giants?

options.text: Joshua drove them out and destroyed them, so none remained in Israel's land except a remnant in a few coastal cities.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 record the destruction of the giants who had once terrified Israel.

options.text: They defeated Israel and forced a retreat.
options.note: The reverse: the giants who once terrified Israel were themselves destroyed.

options.text: They made a peace treaty with Joshua.
options.note: Only Gibeon made peace; the Anakim were devoted to destruction, not treated with.

options.text: They were left completely untouched.
options.note: They were eliminated from Israel's land, with only a coastal remnant surviving.

quiz.q: How is the completion of the conquest summarized?

options.text: Joshua took the whole land as the LORD had promised Moses, and the land had rest from war.
options.note: Verse 23 sums up the conquest as the fulfillment of God's promise, ending in rest.

options.text: Joshua conquered only part of the land and gave up.
options.note: The summary says he took the entire land in keeping with all the LORD had spoken, not part of it.

options.text: The land remained at war for many more generations under Joshua.
options.note: The chapter ends with the land at rest from war, not ongoing conflict under Joshua.

options.text: Israel abandoned the land and returned east of the Jordan.
options.note: Israel took the land as their inheritance; they did not abandon it.

quiz.q: What does the rest that ends the conquest point toward?

options.text: The greater and lasting rest secured by Christ, of which the land's rest was only a shadow.
options.note: Hebrews 4 reads the land's rest as a foreshadowing of the Sabbath rest in Christ.

options.text: That God's promises finally failed to be fulfilled.
options.note: The opposite: the rest shows God's promise fulfilled; it points beyond itself to a greater rest, not to failure.

options.text: That Israel would never face any struggle again.
options.note: Later struggles came; this rest was real but partial, pointing to the lasting rest in Christ.

options.text: That the land itself is the final goal of salvation.
options.note: The land's rest is a shadow of the greater rest in Christ, not the ultimate goal.

questions: Israel faced its biggest, best-armed enemy yet, and God still said, do not be afraid. What overwhelming situation are you facing where you need to hear that same word from God?
God had Israel destroy the horses and chariots so they would trust Him rather than military power. What worldly source of security are you tempted to trust instead of God?
The giants that once made Israel's heart melt were finally destroyed. What long-standing fear or seemingly unbeatable obstacle do you need to bring to the God for whom no enemy is too great?
The chapter repeats that Joshua left nothing undone of all the LORD commanded. Where is God calling you to complete, wholehearted obedience rather than a partial response?
The land finally had rest from war, a foretaste of the rest Christ gives. Where are you weary and longing for rest, and how does the promise of rest in Christ speak to you today?

translationNotes.note: Devoting them to destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem, as the BSB footnote notes; it recurs in verses 12, 20, and 21.
Harden renders a verb the BSB footnote also gives as stiffen or strengthen; the same word describes the hardening of Pharaoh in Exodus.
The foothills render the Shephelah, the western lowlands of Judea, as the BSB footnote notes; the same term appears in verse 16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 11:1-23. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
